ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and cookies — sandwich type with cream/chocolate filling; mapped to FoodEx2 composite biscuit category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for chocolate-filled sandwich biscuit; A000E (biscuits and cookies) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific processed snack food category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 30 g (about 3 sandwich biscuits or one small snack packet), commonly consumed as a school canteen or afternoon snack.

nutrient_source.title: Sandwich Biscuit (Chocolate Filling) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sandwich Biscuit (Chocolate Filling), CR ID F11080053. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Biscuits and Cookies — Analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chocolate sandwich-type biscuits
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry for this specific HPB product. GI of 40 (low) is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chocolate-filled sandwich biscuits and cream-filled cookies, which cluster in the GI 35–45 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 19.47) / 100 ≈ 7.8 g (serving size 30 g, available carbohydrate = 64.9 × 0.30 = 19.47 g). Despite low GI, the high absolute sugar content (31.4 g/100 g) and high carbohydrate density remain clinically significant.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (31.4 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.3 g/100 g) triggers the AHA sugar gate for hypertension risk, independent of sodium; sodium at 280 mg/100 g also contributes moderate sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this snack; the high refined sugar content (31.4 g/100 g) independently elevates blood pressure risk through insulin-driven sodium retention, and the 280 mg/100 g sodium adds further cardiovascular burden — choose lower-sugar, higher-fibre alternatives instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 31.4 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate at 64.9 g/100 g with only 1.3 g/100 g dietary fibre indicates a highly refined carbohydrate profile with rapid glucose absorption potential.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this snack entirely, as the 31.4 g/100 g sugar and 64.9 g/100 g refined carbohydrate content will cause significant post-meal blood glucose spikes despite the estimated low GI of 40, which does not offset the high absolute carbohydrate and sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 12.5 g/100 g (palm oil-based), high refined sugar (31.4 g/100 g), and ultra-processed formulation with likely trans fat residues collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this ultra-processed snack; the high saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g from palm oil), high sugar (31.4 g/100 g), and potential trans fat content from industrial biscuit manufacturing are all independently associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sandwich Biscuit with Chocolate Filling is an ultra-processed packaged snack consisting of two round or rectangular baked wheat biscuits sandwiching a sweet chocolate cream filling. The biscuits are typically crisp and lightly sweetened, while the filling is dense, sweet, and cocoa-flavoured, made from sugar, vegetable fat (commonly palm oil), cocoa powder, and emulsifiers. The product is widely sold in Singapore school canteens, convenience stores, and supermarkets, and is popular among children and adolescents as an affordable snack. Per 100 g, it provides 512 kcal, 5.4 g protein, 25.85 g fat (with an estimated 12.5 g saturated fat from palm oil), 64.9 g carbohydrate, 31.4 g sugar, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 280 mg sodium. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate content make this snack clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Despite an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 40, the absolute sugar and carbohydrate load per serving remains high. The product is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4) and carries high-fat and high-sugar dietary warnings. It is not a source of meaningful micronutrients, providing only trace calcium (15 mg/100 g) and modest iron (1.5 mg/100 g).
common_user_queries: Is Oreo-type biscuit bad for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080053) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, appropriately documented. Saturated fat is estimated from known palm oil composition of similar products and documented as such.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at ~12.5 g/100 g based on typical palm oil-based biscuit formulation; not directly reported in HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this product, and no supplementary approved source was available to fill these fields. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chocolate-filled sandwich biscuits; no exact product match exists in the GI database. This food is classified as western/international in cuisine origin and does not require mandatory Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). No ontology codes were invented; FoodEx2 code A000E is used as the closest defensible biscuit/cookie anchor with 'closest_parent' match type declared.
